speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See now, later I believe it looked like it was some kind of a fence post or something piled up there. I see they tore it all down. They don't have any more ballgames up that way. I have a pretty good diamond. Because every time they knocked it across the creek it was a home run. Laughs

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, we made a little bit and blowed out a few stumps there and made a little ball diamond above the lumberyard in Elk River. That wasn't very big. Almost cleared up just about like a block, you know. About there was pretty, I guess the field was big enough. Had a little grandstand and they must have played there for ten, twelve years I think. As long as the mill ran, you know, all them students from the university come up there and they took, about a dozen of them, you know, for them to work in the mill. And in the yard there, you know, in the summertime. So that made a pretty good team.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So they play the game and then they have the fight afterwards?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes. Afterwards that's when they had the fight. Not the team, but of all the jacks, you know, around there. Like if they played, Bovill played down at, below there, the Blackburn Mill was at, not Clark, but the other town below there.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Sandpoint?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Fernwood?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Fernwood. Fernwood were the Blackburn Mill boats, you know. They had a crew there, the biggest, El Cribble Mill, that were about the same size. And lots of loggers, you know, all kinds of camps there, you know. Them loggers and lumbermen, you know, they're the one they had the fight afterwards. Because they had a lot of moonshine with them and a home brew and they got lit up, you know, before the game was over. And the fight started.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had a scaler there, Jack Donovan, he done, he was the empire, mostly. But they never knocked him out. He, some way, he got by. Of course you know the empire get this, something like him and so on down. And he was the only one. He only had one, you know. One of the, whom they set off.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was it a league then or was it just a team to get together and play?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I guess they had someone kind of in charge of it, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were just like the basketball team on the school, they didn't have nothing to roll with. Because this was all outside, you know. They were not high school. They was the Mill crews and the lumberjacks were the picked out. They were both well, we had two or three that worked on the railroad. They were good, brakemen. They worked damn good. They were in their 20s. They fit right in with them students that come from Moscow. Just right. Same in Potlatch, you know. They had a bunch of students too. I think they used to take a couple of dozen or more students every summer. Some put to work, you know, some in the woods and some around the mills. But they had a promise that every year they took a bunch of them students and let them go to work.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Through the vacations.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because a lot of them was in the forestry school and they'd be in the, more or less, in the woods, you know, in the brush.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But if you put Elk River and put Bovill and Troy together, you could beat Potlatch up. Oh, geez. Bovill and Elk River, they beat them all the time then. The pitching was the worst, you know, to get somebody that kind of... That was hard, you know. They had the conductor in Potlatch, Benson. He died that day four years ago. He was pitching for Potlatch there for, oh, ten years or more. Tom Lenk feller, he done pretty good.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There were no practice, you know. They come right off from work and went playing ball. And kept on working until the next game again and put on the glove and played ballgame. No practice. I never seen them practice any.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What was Camp B like? Huh? What was Camp B like, working there at Camp B when you were there before the war?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that was like the rest of them, I guess. It wasn't much different between the camps. Camp B during the...let's see, during the war, it was in 17. When I left, we were camped only two miles from town, up Creek, that's all. In 17, you see, they first logged out towards Neva or towards Bovill on the railroad. Where they didn't own the ground. They owned the timber and not the ground. They had to log that out first enough up Elk Creek, you know. And they bought it, they owned all of it. Timber and all, so there was no hurry but that timber. That was left to the last, just before the mill went down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The last logging up there, we cleaned it up and they... went to Potlatch, hauled it to Potlatch. In 34 and 5, 1934 and 5, we logged two summers up there and they... they logged all them to Potlatch and they caught them there. The mill went down in 30, you see, in the depression. And then in 32, we logged Potlatch Mill was down. We logged two camps there at...on the Potlatch side. They didn't log any in Clearwater. We had Camp Lebanon, Camp 2. And all them logs went to Lewiston. They hauled them to Lewiston, down to Lewiston, up to Moscow and down to Lewiston. And they said that was the cheapest log they ever had to cut in Lewiston.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Labor was cheap, you know, everybody had their work. 47 cents a half an hour for labor.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That wasn't very much, because we jeopardized a little bit. Oh, they could make five, six, seven dollars some of them. Some of them didn't.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You do hell to be a foreman, I tell you. They were pinching on you so when they choked you. The management. Hold her down, hold her down, hold her down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had run one camp there and my brother run another one. I think we were in...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: ...about three million feet to the camp all summer. About six million. I forget, in the fall when we got through there, I believe we had... ...something like 30 million feet of logs as we were in the two camps. With horses.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Which camps were these? What was the names of these camps?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think there were two and eleven. Camp two and camp eleven. I was in two. They were out there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Let's see what we had to... I was camped on Cloverleaf Creek and my brother, he was up on Oviatt Meadows. That's between Park and Elk River. We took them logs and over I was seven miles from the Elk River mill in Holland to Lewiston.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: From Bovill, Potlatch, Blues, Pullman, Moscow, Kendrick and down to Lewiston.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Only white pine. No other species. Just picked out the white pine. I think only eight or eight felled chalk roads in there and picked out the rest of it. There were jellapines standing there, four feet on the stump and we couldn't touch them. They wouldn't let us. White fern, red fern, tarmac. Just white pine only.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was in the depressants in 32.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: All the mills were down. Most of the Potlatch were down. Elk River was shut down for good.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you first went into the woods, was there any blister rust at all?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I don't know. There might have been. I suppose there was, but they hadn't started that. I don't know really when they started that. If that was started after the... Yeah, I think that started after the war. First World War. I don't think... I never heard of a blister rust before that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The government hadn't started anything in it then. Till later in years. No, at the first time, I never heard of it. But in the... through the 20s there, they had... ...and 30s through the 20s partly. And in the 30s, there were a lot of blister rust camps and kids. My kids worked in it. And during the CC camps, they were blister kids.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever seen it? Have you ever seen that rust on the trees? It's terrible now.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Is it?

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah. It's very bad.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But now they don't seem to pay no attention to it, do they? They've almost given up on their way to find it. It looks like it. They're trying to grow a species that will be resistant to it in these little plantings. And then they're trying to plant them out and see if they'll...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well then, the white pine then will disappear, wouldn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: If they don't take care of it. If they don't even try to. I think in the east, what happened was it got down so there wasn't many white pines left. And then the rust died out and now the white pines are coming back. Maybe that'll happen here too. It could be. It's the same there in Idaho, maybe. I never noticed as much as I did in Marble Creek. In the old lashing there and the new growth come up. And they were just covered, thick, them limbs. With that rust. That's the first time I ever really paid any attention to it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you were logging there in the 30s and just taking out white pine, did a lot of that pine have Lister rust?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, that wasn't affected a bit.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, there was... Up around Elk River, they had camps up there. They had one camp that they usually every summer. They had little small strips that they took care of.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There it didn't bother so much. The first place I seen as really tough was in there on Marble. In that young growth to come up after the 22 fires. The young pine was up and over there. It killed them off pretty much.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you were at Camp B there before the war, from about 15 there, 16, 17, was the logging methods the same? Did they use the same methods as you did when you first came to Potlatch? Or had they changed?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, they only changed the potlatch head in 1907, I think, in the fall. They sold, put in there all their horses and turned into steam donkeys. Then they were temporary till after the war, I think. Or somewhere in there. Then they come back to horses again. And we used horses and donkeys together. They had some camps, you know, and usually three of them steam donkeys. And the horse camp would be probably from 15 to 20 teams in each one.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Or parallel camp would have you, kind of, well, we had one horse camp and two donkey camps for a while. Then we started to mix them up. They had both horses and steam in each camp. That was pretty good.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We'd set up a donkey, you know, for a long haul, a mile haul, you know, and use horses and skid to it. And they hauled them into their track and load them. That was pretty good.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you just had donkeys, did you have to put in a lot more lines?

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Seems like, I mean, you'd have to put in more lines in order to cover the whole area.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yeah, well, each donkey was separate, see. And each one had a setting and if there was a short line, they took a circle of about half a mile. And if they had one of them high lines, what they call it, support on, you go out, you can cross two, three ravines in all our hills. Go out a mile, take in the...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: With the ground line, you couldn't do that. You said you'd have to have a gradual slope. We couldn't go over a hill with the ground line. But the high line, you see, when you come up the hill, you know, they picked the whole load up. And then when it broke over the hill, you know, well then you had to more or less just keep the donkey running and check it with the brakes so it didn't run away on you. Till you get to the next hill, you know, then you had to use more steam again and then you had to just about lift the load up off from the ground and come on over the hill with it. With the ground line, you couldn't do that. We had a spool laying on top of the hill for the cable to go on. Otherwise, you know, we had the cable pretty hard. But underneath that, that's it on the ground to save the cable.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When the load come over there, there was a hanger there and they kind of lift it up. They'd go over all right.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See, when you come to that shoe up there, you know, on the hill, it was uphill. When you dropped over, then it was downhill again. Then it brought the slack over behind you. And there was a little, that'll be a swell. Till you get towards the next pole, then you go uphill with it, drop down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: With that load, you know, sometimes we have a carload hooked on, 12-15 logs or more or less. That's pretty heavy, turns of it. That picked the slack. If there was any slack in that tight line, you know, between the hangers, it'd wear down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now that was, today is a real sport.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When you get out of town, then you had to get away from the noise at the mill. You could hear them donkeys and them three locomotives in the side, they'll stay back in all directions.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Potlatch and Benton and Blackwell. They were the big loggers on the St. Mary's or Elk River line. One time that I counted 21, three locomotives going from Elk River to St. Mary's. Pulling logs into the main line for the Milwaukee to take out. Putting every logger in the train and the rails.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See out of Elk River, we had three of them. Bovill, they had six, I think. Then you got down to Clarkia, you had to Rottweil, they had four or five. Then you got down to Fernwood, there was Blackwell, they had a bunch of them. Six or seven. And the rest of them were smaller, yet they had one. And they usually noosed out, you know, just about the time the old passenger come by, you know, they all was down there and set out logs and picked up empties and went back in the woods again for evening.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: About when was this, that there was all this operation going on at once?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: After they got the rails in there in 1910, from 10 on. That's when everybody started to log. Well, they had, they drove the St. Mary's River little train from Clarkia down. Dumped them in the river, you know, in the creek there up beyond Clarkia Meadows. And they had dams here and there. And up towards Ramida, they had dams. And that, on the Santa Creek, you know, that run into St. Mary's River. And they logged in the wintertime, logged with horses, you know, when the spring talk comes, or they had water, they used the water in the daytime, you know, close to them at night and hold it. Hold it for next morning.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they drove millions of them. But after the railroad come in, you know, then the drives, that all quit. But no more of that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Marlborough Creek, the first few years, you know, they drove that quite a bit until they got the... That was the idea with the Rutledge, you know, they had Marlborough Creek Drive into St. Joe River. Then get them down to Coeur d 'Alene and tow them into Coeur d 'Alene. But after they had that fire, you know, then they had such a rush that they built the incline. That ended the drive, though, no more. So they built the rail over, in and all them, creek down North and then... ...Bustle and... And they could bring the flat cars in there and load them. That ended the log drive on the Marble Creek. They put in the chute there and first that... ...well, not a chute, but a flume. The flumes in there, you know, that burnt up. That all burnt up during the 22. So everything they done in the beginning to hit the logs, that all went in that fire. And after that, then they had to put the incline and get the rails in there.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't more creeks than Marble Creek have flumes on them? Was Marble bigger than the others?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that's the main one, you see. That's all the smaller creeks, they had the chutes, you know, and trailed them... ...trailed them down to the river and there were dams there and there. So they could flute that all summer down into Marble Creek, splash flubes, you know. Let down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But after that, Marble is the main one. All the other creeks not run in just feeders, smaller ones.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, I think they had more flumes there. The diamond mats, no, it went in, I guess, built that mill at San Joe. They had flumes up there, the first creek or two upriver.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And over at Micah Creek, by Calder, between Calder and Herrick, there was a flume that was running there in the 30s. There were flumes in there. They had a little railroad up beyond the Micah Meadows. Little narrow gate, small trees, I don't know what they run, oh probably 20 ton, or some 20, 25 ton trees. Little steam engines on rails, small rails are packed in. Run and haul through the flume there where they had the dam. And over there when I was with the CC camp there at Herrick, they were fluming then. We built a road, we built a bridge over the flume, went under our road in the lower end, near the San Joe River.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And on Marble Creek, the Potlatch had a long flume, or not Marble Creek, but back over there.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Back over there, Herrick or mine, what the heck do we call that creek?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Swamp Creek. Swamp Creek. They had a flume. Big one, long one, that must have been miles of it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You see dams?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That far in between the dams, you know the waters splash out, but the logs come, you know. Then they had feeders, little dams, you know, and they make a little chute flume-like. And pick up the water out of the creek and run it back in the flume again to help out. Otherwise, you know, the logs sometimes get up, you know, and they just be three, four together. And no water run through it, that splash behind them. And the flume get dry ahead. No water. They hold all the water back, you see, and it flushes out, so that's why they had to have them little feeders all along there to help out and get the water back in the flume again.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The log would go a lot faster than the water run, after it gets started.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to see one of those in operation.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes. We had chutes. I never worked on a flume, but we had chutes. We're running chutes, you know, by golly, you know, it's two miles long up the hill there, you know, coming down. Had to have a lookout man, you know, holler, lookout, bellow, when they were coming. And I tell you, they were coming, I mean, they were coming down the road. They were dangerous though. A lot of them get killed around them. They get going so fast, you know, they wouldn't stay in the chute. They'd just take off, leave the chute. It's a curve, you know, they take that over. So a lot of people got caught there. We had goose neck sitting on both sides in the timber. By God, you know, that bark fly in there, I said three when that log shot through, but that'll just slow them down quite a bit. Couple hundred feet, you know, and then another pair of them or two sat there, you know. They were made out of inch and a half square steel, what we call a goose neck. They were, they bore a hole in the side of it, you know, and they were made in just like a goose neck and set it down through there to hold them. And a long shank, you know, against the side there to hold it so they wouldn't, they wouldn't trip back. By golly, you know, that, that'd be a pile of bark there, ten foot high on this item. It's down to their ends.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: I heard that one of the reasons they put the goose necks in was because the logs would get going so fast they'd start a fire.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, well, that could happen, I guess, but they get too fast and when they get too fast, you know, they wouldn't stay in the shoot. You had to slow them down, and you had to have where the landing was. You had to slow them down so they come in there so they could work there. I had, on the landing, one shoot landing there, we had, I think I had sixteen pairs of those to slow them down so they just come running when they come down today. One got stuck, the next one knocked them loose.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because we had a crew of men down there, you know, by God, for that you couldn't keep nobody, you couldn't stay there. You had to come down slow. We had to slow them down somewhere.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In the wintertime it was rough. I had a two mile one there, shoot. And the first thing in the morning, put four horses on and pick out the biggest log you could find. Start from the top and plow the snow out. Snow the foot every night, you know. And just pull that clean down to the landing so then the shoot was clean, they were all shriveled out. So when they were two, then let them come, let them go. If they got going too fast, you had to put in a goose neck and slow them down.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Would that shoot, would it ice up?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, you know, if it was that, you bet they got icy. And the more ice you got on it, the faster the log would go. You bet. Because when the sun come up, you know, they had to all leave. Then they slowed down themselves.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And in the summertime, you know, in order to help them, we put in, they were always near a creek, you know, to get the water in, little water running. And that happened. That was just like grease. Better than grease because a lot of places where there's no water, they use grease and that was dangerous. Then they log, then they could cut fire. You bet.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: The grease would catch?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You bet, you bet.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you use, then, would the horses bring the logs up to the shoot?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Mm-hmm. They shoot and then they just... Get good horses and then places where they shoot to slaughter, trail with horses, shrivel them with horses. Pick a big log and you could shove 15, 20 logs ahead of you and get them over the hill and then they run, go back and get another one, drag. I can put, there'd be a lot of flats, you know, that shoot, be level, you know, for probably three, 400 feet where they won't run over themselves. And they have to have help and you had a team there and push them over.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And some places they use the donkeys to, had holders to hold the cable near the, near the shoot and pull them with the donkeys. You take a string, you know, 50 logs, more or less.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Would they hook on to the first, to the first log? Pull the first one? That'd be the last one?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, that'd be about the last one. Yeah, right. The last one. You push them, see. No, if you took the first one, the first one wouldn't do any good. They only had one then, you see. Go back and get one that's pretty heavy. And then probably, well, you could go back, no, you had on the donkey there, you had several hooks. Go back and get another 15, 20 logs and go back and get another 15, 20 and then start. So you come in with the hundred logs or something and come down to the landing, more or less.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What kind of terrain would make you decide to use a chute instead of just a donkey line?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, some of them didn't, if you didn't have a donkey there, a lot of them didn't have a donkey to spare. Or if you had a donkey there, if you had to have water. And in the summertime, there was the danger of fire. You got to burn wood, you know. And sparks, the sparks had to fire in the place. So in the summertime, they didn't want to use the donkey as much. Just used them in the winter.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were you at that camp? Were you working at that camp on Swan Creek?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No. I was up there a couple times, but no, I never worked at it all.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why they decided to put that chute in?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, there was, yes. There was the only way they could get the logs down. There was no trucks them days, you know. The trucks just started to come in. I think the last log from up in that country too, they hauled them down. They had camp why, they were above, above Dent, above Clearwater.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I think the last logs in from Swan Creek, I believe they trucked them down, down there to the mouth of Elk Creek and dumped them in there. Dumped them in the river.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No chance to get a railroad up in there, you know. Too far. Had to build, build for, road for Orofino up to Swan Creek to get in there. In the first place that caused more than the logs were worth.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The next creek up there, Robinson Creek, you know, and Gold Creek. Diamond Matched logged that and took the logs over to Cusick up here in Newport, Foundry River.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: After Potlatch Mill went down, the all more logs out of there. I think the all more logs out of there since the mill went down and what they cut when the mill was in there. I don't believe they cut a billion feet of lumber on that mill as long as I was running.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I've heard it too, but I don't remember the exact thing, but I don't think they went up to a billion feet. And they hauled out more since the mill went out and then what the mill cut when they were sitting there. So you know, really that mill should have been stayed there and they hadn't been sown there yet. If they had got all the timbers.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was it harder when they didn't have any horses? Was it tougher to log with just the donkeys?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I would think that the horses would be real convenient to pull those logs down to the donkeys. They were. They were. Horses, I think the horses coming back again. I think they're going to start the Forest Service is starting. Trying to get the horses in now to save timber. Save the smaller trees.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The gas power, motor power is too much power.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You take a team, you know, we used to get the team for $200, $400, something like that. Where they got a darn cut at that time cost $6 ,000 or a little better. In a lot of places you could do more work with the team than you could do with the darn cat after you got them. And look at the different investment on them, them days.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was pretty discouraged when they first took the horses away from us and gave us them cats. They'd be stuck sitting in the rain, you know, in steep hills. The cat can't go. Stuck sitting there, mired.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Had to go and get another one, help them pull them out, get them going again. I was thinking that when they first went there they had them cats, you know, boy, if they only had horses. Could have had circuits around them cats.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But you got up on, farther up where you couldn't reach it with the rails, you know, and they had to get something. The longer haul the horses couldn't go. They tried the chutes and they tried the flumes. But it ain't like now when they got the cats and trucks, you know, no matter where it is, you can always build a road up to them. Come up there with the truck and get them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now they use helicopters. And I sat and looked at up about Halsey, not Halsey, but Alsea in Oregon. I saw that sapling sitting there, just like this one you got here, the rubber company showing here at the fair. It's up every night, isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Or every day. That was sitting there, it was going back and forth. So I stopped the car and I said, I find there was a patrol man come along, so I stopped him. I said, what in the heck is that? What's the matter with that doing over there?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said, they're logging. You know, it stayed on the same level and they pick up the logs, you know, and go over to the landing, go back up. He said, it's rocky up there. He said, they can't build no road. There's nothing to get up there. So they bought one of them saplings. But now I see they're using the helicopters. Now they're using them. Him and the other boy, they really can do it. They got the winds on them and they can lift up the log off the ground and go sand them down. Bring that hook back and hook on tomorrow. In the rough timber, you know, if they don't want to cut a tree, you know, but he was standing there. This one hurt him because he picked the log up and go over it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And he's always off on the ground so they never lose him, you know, when they want to get that tight. That's it. And they get him at the landing.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, that'll be it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did you log in the summertime if you couldn't use the donkeys very much?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Horses.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You used only horses then?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Horses. Yeah.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Horses till the cat come and...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: First they cut the long legs, double-length logs, you know, and now then later again they put wind on the... on the cat, you know, and use it to take the whole trees now. Hook on to the top end and take four, five, six, whatever it is. Then they go in with them to the landing and spread them out and they buck them off in the landing where they're loading them. Now they don't even have to have shokers at it. They got a... They got a clamp there on the back end of the power. They go and get a tree and get another one and yard them up five or six together. And back the cat over it and this thing cramps them and takes all of them in a bundle and goes in. The operator on the cat does all of it. The one man can do... When they fall the tree, you know, they'll top them off, cut the top off and leave it to the cat. It's getting there to get the rest of it in. They don't limb them, you know, they come top ahead, you know, by the time they get to the landing there ain't no limbs on them. They're off, pull them off.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you clear cut most of the time? Did you take everything?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We cleaned most everything and we did select the logging tool a little bit, not too much. We done it on one place there, 40 acres I think or 80 acres, whatever it was, I don't remember. When it was through there looked like a park. Before we got done again, two years later I had to go back in and clear cut it. It looked like hell. Take it all. Why?  don't know. Management.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And then they let the river out of there but then the fire got into it. And it got away when they burnt it, burned the brush or something and just killed off everything.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In Sweden they used dress maser. They marked the trees to cut. By God, you know, every six, seven years they could get a pretty good yield that way. We went with horses and picked them logs out of there and saved the trees growing, saved the underbrush, the second road coming up. We just turned them out tonight. That's selective logging. But every few years they got a little crop. Sell off some more. When I was in France they had selective logging. We took what the forester marked for us. By God, you know, he could tell you if you took a stool of tree on them, when the next time you come over he'd go and look and say, I don't know. They laid it out in steps and on and you re-logged the first one and that and that. By God, you know, if I was like for skid roads and so forth the entire one. Once in a while we had to knock out a tree and I'm glad to get the stump out of there to get a road in. By God, he'd know it. When you come up the next time, they'd get a stool of one on them.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that one way is better than the other? Do you think the selective is better than the clear-cut?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I don't know. I couldn't tell you that. It just depends on the management, I guess, whatever they think. First the old little fella out in the field, you know, and then make what's difference what we thought of it. No, you know, now the main idea now is to save timber, to save it. And I don't know, right now it don't seem to make any difference what years ago there was the cost. Now it don't seem to any different on cost because if it costs more there is a price on it. Years ago you couldn't do that. You had to hold down. Now it don't seem to make any difference what it cost. People buy it anyway. Nobody care about prices anymore.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The numbers sky high, you know it. We put that little room on there, you know what that cost is? What it cost me? That ring on there? A few thousand.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Eighty five hundred dollars to put that on. Ain't that something? Ain't that turbine? Don't look like much. That's what I paid for, give me a sec, for eighty five hundred dollars.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, Axel, how did you come to go into World War I?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You told me before that you went up to Spokane to hunt up some men for the operation.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell us about that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I was going down there on the train and I bought a, I got a paper. My name was in it for the draft. But then still I wouldn't have had to go. I was not a citizen, you see. I wouldn't have had to go at all. But the way the things looked, you know, they were in a strike. Nobody working. So I said to hell with it. I'm going to enlist. So I started looking a little bit. At first I was a railroad regiment. That's the one I wanted to go in. But when I got to go down and look, you know, they moved to Portland to finish up. So here come the lumberjack regiment in.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Hey, Axel, wasn't there a story about, wasn't there a story about, you tried to hire some guys? And they wouldn't let you, the Wobblies wouldn't let you go on the train?

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah, I did.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Is that it? Tell us about that first, okay?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that's the same time. That's the same time before I enlisted, you see. I was there. Oh, I must have been in there two, three weeks before that. I tried. Sometime, you know, one time there I must have had about thirty of them. And we got down, down into the front street then, or Trent, in front of the Milwaukee Depot, you see. By God, they wouldn't let us go across. Hell, there were hundreds of them. No use to fight about it. Right, they don't. No, they wouldn't let us get out of town.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Who sent you to get the men? Well, who were you getting the men?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Potlatch Lumber Company. They were Potlatch Lumber Company then. That's before they murdered ten, twelve. The merger was in 31 and this was in 17, you see. So Potlatch Lumber Company then. Now my superintendent sent me in there to see if I could get a crew. We just stayed there until I could get one. And I didn't come back for, for near three years. They took me before I came back yet to that group.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, they followed me, that employment officer man there, he told me, he said, You watch tonight when you go home. I stayed up on St. Nicholas. I think it was up on the 1st. And he said, you step inside the door and he said, you see, just a minute, just get inside, but stay in the lobby. And he said, go and sit down and watch. And he said, there'll be two men walking. Then they'll look all over for you. And by God, they did. It was right. They had me spotted, you see. They were watching me. So I couldn't get no crew. Hell no.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What month was this now?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's in July. I was thrown in, in the army the sixth day of August. This was in July, you see.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had the strike in June, you see.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that kept on. I don't know when they stopped it. I don't know. Because I guess they went out, they agreed to change the conditions in the camp. I think that would stop the strike. It was a good thing they needed that. After that, we had nice clean camps, you bet. Clean blankets and sheets and pillowcases and hope.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: But you gave up the ghost then, huh?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You decided there was no point in trying it. Well, no. It wasn't. I could have stayed there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, I never felt right if I hadn't gone. By God, I wanted to go. I never did. I had a good time. Plenty of hard work, but then, outside of that, it was all right.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So was it tough for you to get in? Hard to get over there?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No. No, I didn't have no trouble. Not at all. I went up to Coeur d'Alene and talked to them. At the Forest Service, I was only in the Forest Service. I got a physical. I had to have five years of recommendation later, two of them, to get in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And it was easy to get to. I worked on the same place. I worked seven years up there almost at Alkribirsee. So I planted out lots of recommendations and done everything that anybody could do in the woods that anybody else ever did. I had done it. No, I had no trouble getting in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: My only trouble was that I should have been a citizen. Then I could have made something out of it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you have some trouble getting those letters, if I remember? Because you were up there and they sent the letter to the wrong place. What was that? Didn't they send the letter of recommendation to the wrong place?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, you just had to get them fast.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that it? You had to get the letters fast. They had to come quickly.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yeah, that was... Oh yeah, that... Oh, I didn't... Yeah, they sent them down to that office in St. Mary's. Because, you know, that was in the St. Joe Forest. And I didn't go there. I never thought of it. I didn't think of that. Well, you know, I went over to Coeur d'Alene to the supervisor's office in Coeur d'Alene Forest. And he didn't have anything. He didn't have nothing. I couldn't figure out. So I said, why golly, it must have been... I said, I had word. I said, I had letters. And they sent them two letters. One from Mr. Ladd and one from Mr. Blum. One come from Potlatch and the other one come from El Cuber. I tried to get a call. The St. Joe Forest called. I said, you got a phone?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, call the office there at St. Mary's. See, maybe they got it. So he called and they said, yes, there's two animals laying here. Been here for a day or two. So I said, you got to go and get them. And I said, no, I'm not going to get them. I said, I made up my mind I'm going to enlist in tomorrow morning. And I said, mine is made up. No, I'm not going to St. Mary's. I'm going to Spokane and enlist. And he said, you can't enlist here. But I said, I start looking enlist. I said, I go in the army or artillery, anything. Any damn thing. But I said, I'm going to enlist. I made up my mind I'm going to enlist somewhere. So I said, if you want me to wait for you, I said, you call that supervisor, Wolf. And I said, tell them to open them letters and read them to you. Read them over the phone. If you're satisfied, okay, well then I'll stick around with you a little bit. If they don't pass, have enough there, well, then it's all off. But I'm going to enlist anyway, tomorrow. So I got him. He did that. I'm going, hell yes, he said, you bet. He said, I got three more sitting there. He said, they haven't got theirs, clearing yet. He said, would you recommend them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And he asked me the names. And I said, sure, you bet your life. I know them. For more than five years, they're good men. You bet. He said, I'll get you their ticket for the recruiting office. So I got them with me to win the Spokane. No, they were all right.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you go then after, where they sent you after?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Washington, D .C. We went to Washington, D .C. for a month, almost a month, after I left Spokane. Done a little training there, gave the squads east and west a little bit, so we could know the right foot from the left, just about all. But we were on the boat, you know. I was on in the sixth of August and the ninth day of September. I was on the boat, going to France. So we didn't have much training.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: What little we did, they had there 120 degrees and no shade. Out in the fields, they were doing a few squad rides. And 120. Pretty hot.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's the hottest place I've been into.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Over at Tucson the other night, first game, when they started the game there at 8 .30, there was 103. He said when they come into the ball diamond, there were 106. When they started the game, there was still 103 at 8 .30 in the evening. That's pretty hot, isn't it? We had 85 instead of warm.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So your unit was all foresters? Forestry people?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah. That was late after we got across the node, we increased it with 100, most of it 250 to the company. But when we first went across the node, all of them was college students. Not me. I was just going through grade school. And you know, they were kind of embarrassing a lot of times and then put me in the yards with them. Get some work out of them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was embarrassing, I tell you. Sometimes I felt I'd like to be any place only not where I was.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I made it alright.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But you probably knew a lot more about the woods than me. I didn't. But you know, they were all college, you know, and up all in the 20s. They had the book education and I had the practical experience. I knew what I was doing. When they had no idea what the hell it was all about. That's the only thing that helped me.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because I knew every bit of it, how to do it, and could do it myself and tried to learn, teach them how to go about it so they didn't get hurt. We never had anybody hurt any friends with that outfit I was with. We were alright.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And any darn thing they asked me to do, I could go ahead and do it. No matter what it was, in the road building, no matter what it was, in anything that had anything to do with logging or roads or anything, I could do it. I was in forestry schools. Deans. First captain I had, you know, he was the dean of the forestry office at the University of Missoula, Montana. He was the dean of forestry there. The first captain. The other one, the second one I was with, the company, he came from, oh he'd been up in Canada. And come on down, his name was Lefouen. John Lefouen. He was pretty good. Pretty good man. But you know, they knew the book. But they didn't know anything about logging or anything. So they left me alone too. They never bothered with me. I could go ahead whatever I did was alright. Sometime I asked them, sometime I didn't. Most of the time I didn't. Just go ahead and use my own judgment.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had mules and wagons to haul logs there and that was pretty slow. The mills, the mill crew, they were pretty damn good, good crew. One of the sawyers used to saw a parrot Harrison.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know, I can't tell you the name of the company but he worked for Wale. At that time there were five big sawmills up at Harrison on the Coeur d 'Ling River there on the lake. They had rails and boats there so they were alright. His name was Bell.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And the others were squat. I don't remember where he came from. I think he came from the south somewhere.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So you know they had circle saws, not bend saws, we had circle saws. Two circle saws, one on top of the other. If they needed them. The timber wasn't very big, you know. I think they were five foot saws. Five foot circle saws, that's the big one.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was there any camp at all set up where they sent you to?

speaker: Unknown Speakers
words: Oh no, no, no.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no, we were in Belton. Oh no, we had a little pop tent which slipped in first. It's under the canvas, each one of us. Oh not all of us had tents, you know. We'd go three, four into each one or half a dozen I guess. Maybe the squad went into each other, I guess that's what they were. Set up tents first and then there was a bunch of Canadians in there ahead of us. Where we come to and they gave us all numbers. So we got numbers and it didn't take long to build a camp, you know. There was timber there, pretty much like our white for fast growing stuff. You know, and they built them, we only had half inch number, cut half inch for the walls. We had two of us, alright, studdings. And two weeks after they put them out, you know, it looked like a picket fence. That lumber shrank so damn much, you know, they were, their cracks then we had to go and put double that again, put something over them cracks. Thai people.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And that lumber was funny, you know, you could cut a lumber piece out of the heart of the log and throw them in the water tank out there and they sunk like a rock.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When it was green, not after it dried out because then it flew to it. When you cut it out of the middle of the log, you know, after it came in and throw it out there in the water, pound it on, down she went, just like it was.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: With that evil.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did you decide where to put that camp then?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Where it was water. We had to have water, you see, we had to look over the ground till we find a place where there was water. That is, the water is the main thing. We could always get the wood, we had to get where. And close to where the timber was, close to where the timber was, because they had bought the timber before, you know, the government had, long before we left the state, they had required the timber and the places to go for each company. We would cut it over. Before they were through, we had 20, we started in with 6. And before they were through, there were 36, we got to be the, they started the 20th after that, 20th. We were in the 10th, the engineers. And then they started the 20th engineers. And they come over, and then they put us in with them, and instead of being the company, when we were, the last year, that in 18, we were the 36th company. 36th company. We were the company when we went across the water, and we would come back the 36th company. 20th engineer, they cut out the tent, you see. Put us in with them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And we had 250 men to the, each company. There were a lot of people. Oh, they needed lumber awfully bad, you know. They needed lumber more than they needed soldiers or ammunition either. Lumber was what they were after. Because they had to build. Everything had to be built, you know. The German had blown out everything. They were so, everything had to be started from the beginning.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Build docks, build railroads. You build a railroad from Port Oak Lane up there to the Coeur d'Alene. Clean across France, Americans said. Put up new depots and big yards.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, you bet too, there was something in there. What it takes for barracks, you know, to build. I was near one of their big camps there. But on the coast, when they would come home. I just went far enough, I didn't have to, I was left on the dock. Take care of the baggage for the company. So I never got to the barracks. But I looked in there, there were 125 ,000 people there, soldiers. That takes a lot of room. And them were built practically overnight, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You remember what town that was, on the coast?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See, we landed in the Harve and we parted with the... hmm. It's on the English Channel. Well, what I'm really more interested in is the town where you were near in the camp, where the camp was.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You mean where the lumber camp was that he worked in?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was at Morteau on the main line, you see. And I was up 50 kilometers up on a branch line. Narrow gauge. 39, I think it was 29 inch gauge, that's what you call it. Narrow gauge. And we loaded the logs on the railroad cars and sent them down. At La Russie. When armistice was signed, you know, the captain was with me, we were out looking over some timber and they blew the whistles and bells were ringing, all the church bells were ringing. And that morning, so it was the eleventh day of November. So he said, what the heck is going on? And I said, I bet you the war is over. And he said, how will we find out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, we got to go back into town. They got a communique on the police station there. There's a blackboard out there. They'll have a marked town. So we'll find out. And here, boy, when he saw that, he said, I got to go. And he left me, he had the car, you see, and the driver. And I was on foot. He said, I got to go down to the company. And here I had just as many men as he did when I was in this town, just outside of it. So I went over and I had a saddle horse when I got to camp. I took that and I went out and I met them. They were coming in, four horse, mule teams, you know, four horse teams come in with saw logs. I asked them to drive to one side. And I said, I want to hit and leave. They got a wagon there. Hit for camp. And I said, you'll have till tomorrow morning. It's o 'clock tomorrow morning, better be in camp. But tonight you take off. And I went and got the whole crew in. I told them the same thing. I said, all right, you're afraid till six o 'clock tomorrow morning. But I said, when the bell rings for breakfast tomorrow morning, be here. For I want to know the reason why. I got never all day to get a hell of a time that night. There was two Swedes, they're Larcen brothers. They didn't drink any. They were from Montana and I gave them a whale borrow and a couple more. I think I had six on them, three whale borrowers. And I said, take them with you and go downtown and see if anybody is laid out. Take them home. Take them home. They got a few. They must have made five or six trips. A few of them are laid out. And I got mixed up with the mayor of the town. Because he was, you know, the mayor of the town there, he's quite a man. Boy, he had that stovepipe hat on and a long split tail coat and an open vest and a nice white, big collar on the neck. Oh boy. And he was pretty good. He was a pretty good spender. We were drinking champagne that night. When I left in the morning at five o 'clock, he was under the table. He was laid out. He was laid out. So, our captain came up a few days after that. So I asked him if we wanted to meet the mayor. I said I'll introduce you to the mayor. And boy, all that mayor praised us. He said he never saw such a fine bunch of men in his life. For the captain. Boy, he praised us. And how we were behaving. And he was sleeping underneath the table. I told the captain, I said when I left him, I said he's a pretty good talker. I said he doesn't mean much, but I said when I left, I said he was laying out sleeping under the table. And we were drinking champagne that he paid for.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I thought the champagne was pretty good. The first time when we come in, you know, we could get a bottle of champagne for a dollar. We hadn't been there two days. Twenty-five francs, then five dollars. Five dollars a bottle. But the first night we were in there, we got them five francs. One dollar.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did the men at the camp do a lot of drinking when they were there?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, you know, they couldn't do a hell of a lot on thirty dollars a month. But they done their part whenever they could.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You bet. They had a good time. I think we all did. All of us. I had a good time. I didn't mind it a bit. A lot of work. And I got a hell of a lot of work out of them rookies, students. I tell you, they worked. But I was right with them. I worked that long with them. I was nowhere in labor. We were all there for the same cause.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had a spy camp there one time. That's the first setting we were on. I had a tent or cabin of my own where I slept all alone. So one Sunday I was sitting there and here come a soldier. He was trailing and he had a rifle on his shoulder and another rookie walking with him. This fellow was sent up there for me for punishment. He was restricted to camp for three weeks. Compliment from the captain, you know. So I returned the favor and I said, okay, you can go back. I looked at this fellow. He was over here from Metaline Falls, north of Spokane. His name was Wood. I said, you son of a gun, you think that I'm going to stay home here because you come up here? I said, no. He said, what are you going to do? I said, you're going to go with me. I said, you and me, we go out together. He said, where? I said, where is the cafe out there?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Little Wayne is just out of town. I said, we go up there and have a feed and we get a bottle of beer and we got wine. But I said, don't you leave me. You stay right with me. I said, that's how only punishment there is. So we were filled with chumps after that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: After a war, he come up and worked with me for a year or more. A lot of them after I got back there, they had to cover the first, I mean, in 19 and 1920. I think I must have had two dozen or more of them soldiers come and work with me there. Hunted me up.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did the officers mind the fact that men would go out around the town and stuff? The captains?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I don't think so. No, no, I wouldn't think so. They did it themselves. Had to. We were all in the same boat when you got, if you stop thinking about it a little bit. There were no different ones. Had a different rank than the other one. The best chum that I ever met there was an eager. That was when we were coming home. We were laid there on the dock there for a month and I took a hike out of town, out of the dock. Got in the cafe out there. He was a first lieutenant. Boy, when he had a drink after that, he bought all the drinks. I never did see him anymore. We left about two o 'clock that night. I had to go back home where I was. I think he had to go out to the He was a nice young man. A nice fellow. No, they were all right.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: One time I stayed over there. I had been up for commission for examination and didn't get it. Coming back and I stopped at the town of Bonanza little bit. I had a strange strain. I met a fellow there. We enlisted together over here. I hadn't seen him. He was in E Company. I was in D.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He was in the hospital there. But he was downtown. I went with him up to the hospital there. We had feed there that evening. He got a pass, overnight pass. I had one more day on mine left. He got an overnight pass. We forgot all about it and we stayed two nights. He had an explanation to do when he got back to the hospital. I had to do something when I got home to my camp.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Just when I got there, the first sergeant came and said, the captain wants to see you. There was a first lieutenant that took my place up in the spy camp. His name was Hodgeman. I had been gone eight days. I had seven days. I had gone eight. He said, Sergeant, what happened? He said, you're late. I said, no, I don't know. I said, nothing happened really.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, I missed the train. My friend said, no good. I can't talk very much. He said, yes, yes. I got on the wrong train and I had quite a time to get back again. I said, I'm here. I'm sober. I'm all right. No harm done. He said, all right, all right, all right. He said, go ahead. He said, you better get to the capital as soon as you can. He said, Hodgeman wants to get the hell out of there too. He said, he don't like it. I said, okay. That was all I ever said about it. But I took, I had only, it's only there. It's over. He come home under guard. I know that. I know that. One time that he was out to now and he couldn't find us. He was lost altogether. I don't think he could talk to any friends. He couldn't find any train. He didn't know anything. So they picked him up and brought him back home. He come home under guard. Two soldiers brought him home.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I knew that. I thought I'd work on that train business a little bit.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That worked.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, in the Army you never, never ask for an excuse. Never try to excuse yourself. Just tell them, sure, sure thing you're wet. I got pretty well licked up on time there with the Canadians. Once I was never a payday day. Paymaster come around. And I got, I got home. In the morning, you know, here first thing in the morning, here come the first sergeant and say the captain wants to see you. I said, okay. I had my fatigue clothes on, you know, not the uniform. I had my, just the overalls and the jacket on. But I washed, I guess washed up probably and they went over. He said, sergeant, he said, I expected the sergeant to set better example for the men. I said, Captain, I said, you're referring to me now?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, yes. I said, I tell you, Captain, before I said, you know, I drank more champagne than any four of your soldiers put together last yesterday. I said, I just drank every damn thing that come my way. But I said, I made it home all right under my own power. Yes, yes. Oh, he said, go on, your son.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And every time I met him, he said, go on, go on. Every time I saw him after that, you know, he said, go on, your son.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, never had never asked for any excuse. Make it worse than it was. I learned that much in the army. Trying to excuse, you know, that never worked because they didn't find out anyway. So when he said about drinking, you know, I tell him, I drink plenty. But I made it home. I said, sure, yes, yes.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I put him to bed. That was later, though. Him and I were out scouting, looking over the campground. New place to go. And we were at the hotel there, you know, we were getting in the dining room. And had supper. When they fed, you know, Frenchmen never drinks water. They never drink water. They put a bottle of wine on or a pitcher of wine on the table. First thing. And then you ordered the pitcher of milk. And you, that pitcher never get empty until you leave. That's full until you leave. They fill it every time. So I kept filling his glass. I don't have food into his glass. He said, what?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We said, we didn't order that wine. I said, no, that goes with the meal. That part of the meal. I said, whether you drink it or don't drink it, I said, you pay for it just the same. But that, I guess, is the custom. So he, I guess he liked it. And I kept filling it that long. And the waiter's there, you know, just as soon as I got down a little bit, he was right there and filled it up to the top again. And when he started to walk from the table, by God, his legs wouldn't carry him. So I got the helper there and we got him up to the room. After that, you know, he was pretty good. I had no trouble with the captain after that. Because only him and me, that was all there was.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I did the same damn thing there in the Ratine camp. We were three sergeants. We went over to the cafe after retreat. We were laying around the camp. And the man there at the cafe said he just got in a load of wine from Bordeaux. He said, that's good wine. So we bought a bottle of peace and you get three glasses out of a bottle, you see. Three ordinary water glasses were put out of a bottle. So they opened one and we each wanted that glass and drank it. And each one bought a bottle. We were fine. We went home. And we all slept in the same barrack, you see. All the sergeants were together. And along about midnight or so, I heard something and I woke up and I looked and there was a mess sergeant. He was one of them. His name was Kevin. And he crawled on the floor and out through the door he went where he was on the hands of his niece. When I paid in the hotel, I thought it was all right for fun here. That he wouldn't fall. But I thought he had to get out anyway. A little bit to know here, I heard it again. There was the other one. His name was, he was out here from Centralia. Hubbard. He got killed. The Wobblies shot him out there in Centralia later after we came home. And he took off. When I raced up, by God, now I had to take off when I raised my head up. And I was there and I had to go to where I was heaved. And my legs were no good. So I took that after him before. Then, you know, we sat out there till the Rabelie. Six o 'clock when the Blue Rabelie. We never got back in again. And we heaved everything else. And I haven't cared for wine since. That done me unwind. The head was all right. We were cleared. We were sober. That's good. That's when you come home. But I just took my stomach and boy, all three of us acted the same way. We just had to throw up everything that was in us when we sat there for two o 'clock. For four hours, I guess, we sat out there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was on, I think that was sweet wine, if I remember. But that was that one they sent me. When they were wine, I said, nope, I don't want no wine. None. Well, you got to go now for the zone?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, we can stay a while more. Unless you're going to kick us out. Well, no, I'm not going to kick out. We forgot the Watergate. Well, just as well. See what they're going to do. I think they just talk too much. It seems like they're just sitting there talking and doing nothing. Shooing the same thing over and over. Or you'll find a creek that was deep enough to jump in.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where could you take a bath in town?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The barbershop. The barbershop always had a bathtub. No showers. I thought the kids used to say, what do you do for a bath?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, we didn't. We didn't get any. Oh, I wouldn't stay there. I said, you stayed, you bet you stayed. You had to eat. So I said, and if you didn't stay any place, you didn't eat, eat?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, no, I didn't. I was 10 ,000 miles behind me. So I couldn't go home. I wasn't like you could go home to your dad and sit at the table and eat. I said I was on my own, so I said I had to eat, and I said I stayed. Who were you sleeping out in a straw stack?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I went out dressing.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The creeks wasn't deep enough, so you can take a swim in them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But that was tough. That's what Wobblies did, you know. They got showers in the camp. Then in 1918, that was a good thing.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were those hot and cold showers that they got? Or was it just cold showers?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh no, oh no, oh no, they had hot water. Oh, I had a little boiler then. Oh no, it was hot and cold, so you regulate it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they put off pretty good. An extra car, put it up in a washroom and tubs, you know, so they go in there and wash their own clothes and have hot and cold water. Before that time, you know, you had to go to the creek. I tried it one time and had a union suit. I tried to wash it, and when I was through, my thumbs went through all of it. I was nothing but holes. It was like I said, nothing left, and they wasn't clean either. Didn't have no washboard or anything, you know. There were no washing machines in them days. That was at Helmer. So I went down and found a washer woman there one Sunday, and she did my washing for me. You want to stop there and talk to Clay Gustin, the inn. That is a good old bloke. He could tell you lots about LatahCounty and about Potlatch.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was around him, I guess, from 26 to...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: ...in the 40s. I think when he... I know he goes out even now when they spray. After that, I believe he worked on the highway or something, didn't he, for the county? Didn't he work on the road out to park?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I know he goes out even now when they spray.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Oh, does he?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He and Carl Lancaster work together.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you came back after the war was over, was much changed in the camps besides the conditions?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, it was eat all day. All day then. There wasn't daylight to dark anymore. It was about 10 hours, it was eat.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was that the Wobblies doing too?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, yes. Two nuts, big strike. They got to eat all day in, and they got clean sheets and blankets for their beds.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did it make your crews happier to have this stuff?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yes. Oh, you bet. Sure it did, but a lot of them took... It was in the moonshine, the homebrew days.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They didn't show the appreciation too much, that's for sure. Hit with the muddy cork shoes, get up and get on into bed with shoes and clothes and all in a clean sheet. That don't show too much appreciation. But that's something you had to expect.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You had to expect that it wasn't all roses.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How was the moonshine out there in those days?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, they paid $10 a gallon. They brought it to the camp.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And somehow it was strong, and somewhat diluted, I guess. I don't know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They watched for the foreman to go and leave Saturday evening. They'd come in from Deere, there was somebody from Deere, pop in. And say, Lord, it's Sunday when I come back to the camp. They were sitting around the stump there singing the weed.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And a yoga moon shrine.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But that was a cure for that too. One morning there, one morning I took seven teamsters. And down the road, after that you could see a yoga moon shrine in every manger in the barn. There were 20 teams, nobody drunk. Everybody on the job Monday morning. I told them if they had to have that, down you go. That's all the rest of it. No mocha business. That happened. The only thing you do to scare a waitress is send them out. Send them down the road.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You fired them because they were drunk Monday morning, right?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, they couldn't go to work. They wasn't able to go to work. So that was all of it. I don't like it. I went in old form in there for rope and hauling or the next camp to me that one time. They called him Wood 'em Up George. He had a saloon in Spokane later. He said, raise your wings. He said, and flee for the valley. Hit the office. Raise your wings, he said, and flee for the valley.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He was a very old bastard, I guess. He'd come out and say, hey, you got a partner?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, so-so. Take them with you in here for the office.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, or the heck of the clerk wants to see you. Take them with you in here. The voice says, clerk wants to see you.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That old Wood 'em Up, you know, he was quite a character, I think.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think I heard he got his name because he wanted to always get that last log on top of the car. He always wanted to get the last one out of the good.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, we had the Pilton, the U .S. Pilton, the old man Pilton, Homer's dad. He run camp, camp eight. That was a long time when I first got to know him. But later, you know, he come up and with us there at Alkuber. And I gave him a different job on a donkey. U .S. was with him. He was, I think he blew the whistle. He was pretty old then. And Todd, or Adrian, he run the donkey. He run the machine. Homer was the hook tender. And Lotus, I think he was hoisting. We had a little steam gelly out there and picked the logs up and put them on the car. And there was one more, I forget his name. I can't think of it. Well anyway, you know, Adrian was married. And I put up shacks there for them so they all, we called it Piltonville. They had a cookout shack and another shack to eat in. The cookouts were all there supplying. And we were right near the creek so they dipped the water on the creek and took it in. Nice stream of water. Nice cool water. Well anyway, they start at night, you know, they were logging. Then kicking and hauling and so forth. And the old man told them, he said, if you do half, no, if you do half as much logging when you're on the job as you're doing in the camp, he said, we would make lots of money. He said, we're doing all the logging in the camp. And he said, I don't bring in any logs. There was all these boys, you see. I thought I was a pretty good hag. Yeah, he said, if you do more of that logging in the daytime, he said, and then go to bed when you get home. He says, we're going to make lots of money. They had so much to house, you see. For the 8 ,000 feet on the car.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now we call that the Piltonville. It was just the spot where the family had to operation. Yeah. Close to where the donkey sat. He was doing the cooking for the family, you see. Adrian's wife, she was doing the cooking. For all of them. They were five or six. The rest of the crew stayed in the camp, that's what they had with. There were 21 men to that crew.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: They put in a lot of timber there, didn't they?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: A lot of wood. Oh, you bet. You bet, yeah. You bet.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Some days they had good luck and some days they didn't. Some days they break down or something good old. Lots of time, you know, they start on a new road, you know, they pick up. Six to ten or twelve carloads. Then when they got farther out, the longer out toward the Mile Hall, you know, they couldn't get so many. That for sure, you know, it would take too long to make the trips. When they were shorted in by the donkey, they would pick up a lot of them.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well, during the prohibition, there was no saloons open, right?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So guys would have to drink it. Yes. Yes, that's right. There were no saloons. Although, in the... I had a family. I had a car. I drove around. If I had a Sunday off or something, if Sunday that I didn't get cut, have to go someplace or do work someplace, I took the family and we drove out, you know. One Sunday we went down. I think we went down around Harvard. Yeah, down to Derry and Harvard. And we come back up towards the St. Mary's River. Went up down to Hotlet and come up to Harvard and come out of Midas and Fernwood. And I stopped in Clarkia, never Sunday afternoon. It was pretty warm. They had an ice cream joint there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I went in and I asked the kids what they wanted and they said I brought it out to them. I knew there was a bootleg joint. So I didn't take the kids in. I brought it out to them. They sat in the car. Now I said the family took care of it and I said, now I need some. He said, what do you want? A whiskey or beer?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I didn't go to his place. And he didn't know me from Adam.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I told him I'll take one of each. I'll take a drink of whiskey and a beer for chaser. So there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And one time there in Elk River, you know, I'd already been out with the Snoop Law. In the winter time it was cold. Oh, it must have been about one o 'clock. That was long before that. Must have been in 14 or 13, somewhere in there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: This fellow, we call him Hamburger Schmidt. He's an old cook. He had built a little hotel. He must have had a few rooms up there. I never seen them. And he had a bakery and a restaurant underneath the counter. And I knew he was selling moonshine, you know, selling drinks. And I come in cold. I'd been on that open Snoop Law in the night. My knees were shaking. So I got in through the door and here was the gun. Down there, but he sat on top of the counter. And I let spoke bad and I stood there with the mouth open like an old fool, you know, kid. So the deputy said, what do you want?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I said, you know what I've left. I have gold. Okay. He stretched over this way and underneath it. Here it is, you see, they stuck up a quarter of his. Heh, heh. Heh. But he didn't take no money. He didn't take no money. I took two drinks out of it and then, but he never asked to get it. But you know, when that deputy sheriff sat there on the counter, I was, I thought I was in your own place. But I told him I was cold and I said, you know what I need. Just leaned over and reached underneath and said, come up and have a quart of whiskey.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you drink with your men at all in the, in the?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In town, yeah. In town. Oh, sure.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, you got in, I was, one, I just thought I was just as good as they were.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So why shouldn't I drink with them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I figured they were just as good as me. When we were in town, we were alright. We're not on the job.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's why, when I, you were talking about the Jackets brother down there, Mike and Joe. If I come in there, I knew everybody in there and by God, I could hardly ever get out of there again. So the last few years they ran. I didn't go near too many. And I could just sit down there at the counter and by God, there was, there was stubby sitting all around me. I couldn't encounter them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was, I saw him two years ago, Joe.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And he was telling me that the slow investment was no good. He said it's no good anymore. He said to make no money and make no money. And I said, you got a lot of money. What's the, you don't need it. Yeah, money. He said, no, we shouldn't have it. I said, we can't sell. And I said, give it to the bartender. Give it to him. Turn it over to him. There's no money. He said, nobody got any money. They can't pay. And I said, turn it over to him and let him pay you so much. I said, let him pay you whatever he can. I said, you don't need the money. You can't take none of it with you when you die. So I got three weeks later, you know, I heard he did turn it over to the bartender. I haven't seen him since. Don't know where he is, but I heard he got married.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was over at some funeral up on the North Side there. That's the last time I seen him.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Pat Malone, Axel?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, you bet your, bet your wife. I know Pat. Quite a lad.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now, he had a deputy there, deputy sheriff there in Bovine. Well, I guess till he died. He must have been there for 20 years or more. He was telling me he used to be a policeman in West Duluth.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I think I was there some time. Oh, I was there a lot of times in West Duluth. But you know, they picked the policemen. You know, you could look up the sidewalk and you could see them helmet above all the rest of them. They were that tall. There were no small men there. All big, Irishmen. Damn near all of them. You could see their head above all the people walking on the sidewalk when you could look up the street. You could see them had black helmets. And Pat was one of them. Pat Malone.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever tell you how he came to come out here?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No. No, I don't know. I don't know how he did. I guess he was just like the rest of us.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't think he, see, he was, he didn't follow the lumberjack or anything like that, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He was a law officer. So I imagine he just come out and stayed.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was Pat as big a drinker as they say?

speaker: Axel Anderson
